quote: I don't know if I want it or not. I just wanna find out what it cost.
stewart_read: This is the call's highest-leverage moment and it was walked past. The customer has handed the rep a price-shopping frame — which is also a cost-anxiety frame. The codex's objection_inversion move is directly applicable: the flip on 'I just want to find out what it costs' is to redirect toward what the current bill is already costing. Strategic read: rep could have been deliberately deferring the flip until after bill data was in hand. Reactive read: rep said 'No worries' and moved to logistics, with no evidence of a planned later move. The reactive read holds — the flip never came.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep was deliberately deferring the emotional anchor until after the Dominion call, when actual kWh data would make the flip concrete.
coaching_implication: Show Parker the objection_inversion pattern. The customer's 'find out what it costs' line is the exact trigger. Suggested reframe direction (not verbatim): acknowledge the question, then redirect to what the current bill is already costing them — before any solar number exists. The flip doesn't require kWh data; it requires the $300/month figure the customer volunteered at 01:12.

quote: The the bills average around that about 300 a month.
stewart_read: This is the first instance of the 'ask + acknowledge + move on' pattern the codex flags as the universal floor failure. The customer volunteers $300/month unprompted. Parker's response is to ask for kWh data — procedurally correct, emotionally inert. The $300 figure is the emotional anchor for the entire pitch and it was treated as a data point to move past.
coaching_implication: This is the first flip opportunity. Manager should replay this moment with Parker and ask: 'What does $300/month mean to this customer over a year? Over the life of the system? What would you say if you wanted him to feel that number before you moved on?' The codex's capture-and-flip pattern starts here, not at the end of the Dominion call.

quote: I think the only thing we needed from you, mister Simonson, was your your electric bill.
stewart_read: Parker skips from address confirmation directly to bill collection, bypassing the Verify section. No interest-in-solar question, no title/co-owners check, no roof age, no residence length, no prior designs question. Of the eight Verify items, roughly three are captured across the call (address, bill average, utility company). The codex requires 6-of-8 for completion. The co-owners gap is the most consequential miss — if a spouse or adult child is on the title, the appointment is structurally vulnerable.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have had prior qualification data on file from a previous touch, making some Verify items redundant. The transcript gives no indication of this — the intro references only 'a request here that you were looking at solar.'
coaching_implication: Manager should establish whether Parker had prior data on this lead. If not, the Verify skip is a training gap — walk through which items were missed and why the co-owners question in particular cannot be skipped on any call.

quote: I got it down here for us, mister Simon. So thanks for taking the time. That'll help us get it started for you.
stewart_read: This is the second instance of the 'ask + acknowledge + move on' pattern. Parker has just received 13 months of kWh data including a January reading of 2,254 kWh and a February reading of 3,498 kWh — the kind of winter-peak data that makes the bill-flip concrete and personal. Instead, he thanks the customer and pivots immediately to booking. The usage data is filed as administrative input. Note: the May and June readings in the transcript appear truncated or garbled by Deepgram transcription ('78' and '26' respectively) and cannot be used as reliable baselines — the winter figures are the reliable anchor points here.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have been deliberately saving the emotional anchor for the closer, keeping setter scope clean. However, the codex is explicit that the bill must be flipped at the setter stage to create curiosity — deferring it entirely leaves the customer in price-shopping mode through the appointment.
coaching_implication: This is the second and final flip opportunity. Manager should ask Parker: after you got those winter numbers from Dominion, what did you do with them? The answer should be: connect them back to the $300/month average the customer mentioned, note the seasonal swing, and use that to create curiosity about what the design will show. No fabricated savings numbers needed — the customer's own usage data is the leverage.

quote: and they'll have you just give them a few bits of information, and then they would give you they would read off their your kilowatt usage for the last year. Would that work for you? I can add him to the line real quick.
stewart_read: This is the call's clearest positive moment. The customer is paperless and auto-pay — no bill to photograph, no login offered. Parker pivots immediately to a three-way Dominion call rather than burning time on a dead-end path. The pivot is clean, explained briefly, and the customer agrees without friction. This is competent bill-collection problem-solving.
coaching_implication: Reinforce this move. Parker found a workable path when the standard bill-collection route was blocked. Manager can use this as a positive anchor in the 1-on-1 before moving to the gaps.

quote: We'll plan to just have our specialist drop by tomorrow at noon, and he'll just kinda answer any questions you might have and show you the price and and the savings and see what we can do to help you out there.
stewart_read: The button-up is missing four of six required elements: the one-hour block statement, the phone-appointment specification, the no-driving/at-computer anchor, and the 'is that fair' verbal handshake. 'Drop by' is ambiguous language for a phone appointment — worth clarifying in the text follow-up, though there is no evidence the customer interpreted it as in-person. The framing 'answer any questions you might have and show you the price' reinforces the transactional frame rather than building anticipation. The appointment is booked but the confirmation is thin.
coaching_implication: Walk Parker through the button_up checklist item by item against this call. The missing anchors — especially the no-driving/at-computer requirement and the 'is that fair' handshake — are the ones most predictive of no-show. The text follow-up is the recovery opportunity: it should clarify phone format, confirm noon, and include a tieback to something specific from the call.

stewart_read: The Dominion data reveals a customer whose usage swings dramatically — January at 2,254 kWh and February at 3,498 kWh against a stated average of $300/month. This is a high-consumption household with significant seasonal variation, which is exactly the profile where solar ROI is strongest. Parker received this data in real time and did not use it. The customer's own utility data is the most credible bill-pain evidence available — more credible than anything the rep could assert — and it went unacknowledged.
coaching_implication: This is the moment where the flip becomes concrete and personal. Manager should ask Parker: when Tina read off those February numbers, what did you think? The coaching goal is to get Parker to see the usage data as emotional leverage, not just system-sizing input. A suggested direction (not verbatim): acknowledge the winter peak to the customer, connect it to the $300 average, and note that the design will show what those months specifically could look like with solar. No fabricated numbers — the customer's own data does the work.

quote: And now the last thing, Andrew or mister Simonson, is this a good day to show you that design and kinda go over the price with you?
stewart_read: Parker books the appointment without ever asking who else is on the title or who else would be involved in the decision. The codex treats this as a hard required item — if a spouse or adult child is on the title, they must be on the appointment. Parker has no information either way. The appointment is booked with an unknown decision-maker structure.
coaching_implication: Before the closer walks into this appointment, someone needs to know whether Andrew is the sole decision-maker. The text follow-up is the last opportunity to surface this. Manager should use this moment to reinforce why the title/co-owners question belongs in Verify, not as an afterthought — a solo appointment with a co-owner absent is a structural vulnerability regardless of how warm the customer is.
